BUSINESS MODEL Driven by design, quality and creativity H&M Group’s brands are united by the ambition to make fashion and design available to everyone. With a flexible and strong business model and a broad brand portfolio, we continue to drive the fashion industry forward through innovation, entrepreneurship and customer focus. H&M Group is a global family of fashion and lifestyle A seamless customer experience brands. Our ambition is to give people all over the world Our creative design teams create collections that meet the opportunity to express their personal style – without trends and customer needs. Our omni-channel model compromise. Through our purpose Liberating fashion blends physical and digital s hopping to create an in for the many we challenge restrictive norms and exorbi- spiring, smooth and seamless customer experience. tant prices, all while working to minimise our impact Our stores are more than points of sale – they are meeting on people and the environment. places where people can be inspired, explore their style Our business idea – fashion and quality at the best and experience our brands in a personal way. price in a sustainable way – is the basis of everything We are continuing to invest in both physical and digital we do. Total Scope 3 0.0 0.353 0.0 39.43 Jon Lord | Alamy Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 91 Decarb Decarbonizing Our Supply Chain The climate crisis is an existential threat, and every part of Patagonia’s business is implicated. The bulk of our emissions —nearly 99%—comes from our supply chain. We take responsibility for all of it, so decarbonizing our supply chain is a crucial part of reducing our emissions. Our work on this helped jump-start an industry-wide conversation around thermal energy needs and Up to 75% of the energy that’s generated in a textile mill electrification, and we quickly wanted to follow up with more comes from burning fossil fuels on-site to create heat and tangible mill-level research and solutions. process steam, pressurized steam (typically generated from boilers) that provides the energy required for textile In 2024, we took what we learned from the study and helped manufacturing, including dyeing and drying fabrics. By 2020, our leading fabric suppliers had eliminated new labor fees in our supply chain—a meaningful change we helped bring about that’s now saving approximately 3,000 workers in our supply chain a total of $1.7 million per year. Forced and bonded labor was identified as another material topic in our Double Materiality Assessment (page 11), and we’re now working on a large-scale effort with a coalition of other brands to seek reimbursement of recruitment fees paid by workers prior to 2020 and to push for legislative solutions. In 2022, we conducted a survey of over 1,600 migrant workers in Thailand and Taiwan; only 3% of workers owed money in labor fees and, in both countries, a large majority were hoping to renew their contracts. Through our annual audits, we continue to seek feedback from workers on their experiences and validate that they do not pay fees. “We’ve worked to improve conditions supplier by supplier,” Nguyen says, “But that won’t change the whole industry. We need legislative support. Since labor fees are legal in these Employees at the Sheico wetsuit factory in Thailand. countries, it’s important to keep pushing for local laws that Ryan ‘Chachi’ Craig say no more fees, period.” Our work on migrant worker fees helped save 3,000 workers an annual average of $1.7 M in recruitment fees Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 77 Our Pollution Problem In FY2025, we focused on eliminating “forever chemicals,” reducing our carbon emissions, and cutting the textile waste our products create— whether that’s when they’re made or at the product’s end-of-life. Our Head of Environmental Impact Kim Drenner and Supply Chain Environmental Impact Program Manager Eric Cheng inspect a coal boiler at De Licacy Industrial Co., Ltd., a textile mill in Taiwan.

Josué Rivas Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 38 On the insurance front, we reevaluate policies every year and prioritize carriers with fossil fuel exclusions and decarbonization commitments. We’ve replicated our banking criteria with our corporate insurance purchasing process, where we now require our insurance companies to submit sustainability policies and targets with their annual bids for Patagonia’s business. We meet annually to review progress and push for more ambitious targets. In 2019, we added a new 401(k) option called The Cleanest Line Fund, so employees can choose to invest their retirement savings in ways that match their environmental values. In 2024, we started a new finance program to help our finished goods suppliers get paid faster on Patagonia invoices. It offers better terms to factories that do well with environmental practices, worker treatment and product quality, and encourages other supplier partners to improve. These steps reflect a broader shift in how we think about money—not just as a tool for business, but as a way to support people and the planet at every level of our operations. For anyone asking how to start this process, we can say this: Begin with one account.

Mechi Fahs Intro Business Unusual Product Grants & Giving Activism Work in Progress Report 25 B-ing in Business for the Planet We became a Certified B Corp in 2011 and California’s first legal benefit corporation in 2012—on the first date California law allowed it. In doing so, we adopted six legal commitments that guide how we operate: Donating 1% of sales to environmental groups Building the best product with no unnecessary harm Conducting our operations without unnecessary harm Sharing best practices with other businesses Operating with transparency Providing a just, equitable and supportive work environment To certify, we selected B Lab as the third-party assessor. When we became a benefit corporation, we formally wrote into our corporate DNA that we would prioritize the planet before profit—an early precursor to our new corporate structure. Recertification happens every three years through B Lab’s B Impact Assessment, which measures a company’s impact on workers, community, environment, customers and our governance practices. Since 2013, our B Corp score has increased year over year, from 107.3 to now 166 out of roughly 250 (the median score is 50.9). Since 2013, we also have published an annual B Corp report on our website (check out our past reports here). In 2016, we began closing all US locations on Election Day—a first in company history—so all employees could make it to the polls. In 2024, we shifted that closure to national Vote Early Day.

Holdfast Collective owns 98% of the stock and receives an equivalent cut of the dividend; the other 2% of dividends goes to the Patagonia Purpose Trust for operating costs and climate-related philanthropy. Combined weight (in pounds) of all On average, 85–90% of the products we put out in the world finished garments do not have an end-of-life solution. This is because our products contain mixed materials. A 3-layer waterproof rain 321,000 jacket, for example, has an outer fabric, a waterproof/ breathable membrane and a fabric backer sandwiched together with adhesive—three different materials potentially made of different fibers. Recyclers today are built to recycle one material type at a Number of pullovers produced time, so the complex product we have built for functionality and durability does not readily enter these processes. This is 395,000 an industry-level challenge; it isn’t vastly different from other apparel brands that create similar product. We’re actively working on potential solutions. In 2022, we commissioned a study of textile mills in Taiwan, China and Japan to help identify how feasible it would be to go electric in the entire textile mill with renewable energy and Hard the best pathways to get there. We were the main sponsors; Burton, New Balance, REI, Gore and the Outdoor Industry Alliance (OIA) joined to increase the geographical scope as well as the financing. In the study, conducted by researchers from Global Efficiency Intelligence and Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, we looked at 40 mills across these three countries and found that shifting to industrial heat pumps versus conventional fossil fuel usage could mean significant energy reductions, reduced CO2 emissions and lower costs.

Imagine if we could eliminate the need for packaging altogether? This is the challenge we pose to our Inventors and R&D teams, whose legacy has given us the shampoo bar, massage bar, naked deodorant, henna brick, naked body conditioner, naked shower gel and more soap than you could ever dream of. Naked products also tend to last longer than their packaged counterparts because they are more concentrated and have minimal water content. Tests with users tell us that one shampoo bar can last the equivalent washes as three 200ml bottles of liquid shampoo. Not only are we saving those three bottles worth of plastic, we are also having to ship less product and less weight, reducing C02 emissions. The full report will take you through how we arrived at these estimations. Our initial interest in making naked products was born out of necessity. After the demise of Cosmetics To Go, the Lush Co-Founders simply didn’t have the money to invest in packaging, but this actually sparked a revolutionary approach to a growing industry problem. We have been selling solid products even in the years before Lush, when we were known as Cosmetics To Go. However, they were not born naked!

Principle: Recycled Materials Evolving Principle: If we can’t make everything naked, then the next aim is to ensure that those products that do need packaging use the best and most innovative minimum impact, recycled or reused materials that are available. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 67 PRINCIPLE: RECYCLED MATERIALS When packaging is absolutely necessary, we make it count. We've been using recycled plastic since 2008 in the UK. We recently switched the colour additives from virgin to recycled plastic for black pots in the UK and are rolling this out to other markets, so we can claim 100% recycled pots. Most of our pesky pumps, virgin until recently, now have 30-60% recycled content. Perfume spritzers, Shield & Willow and the 100ml Super Milk are still a work in progress. For our 100% recycled PET bottles, we went further and sourced Prevented Ocean Plastic for most markets (not in North America yet). This comes from bottle collectors working 50km from water bodies, helping prevent bottles reaching the ocean. Labels, despite our best efforts, remain unfortunately virgin.Our gifts make up for it with recycled paper, cotton rags, agriculture waste like banana fibre, knot-wraps from recycled bottles, and ribbons from plastic bottles in use.

Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 32 PRINCIPLE: COMMITMENT TO TRANSPARENCY AND EXTERNAL AUDITS If you're going to make bold claims about your ethics, you'd better be prepared to prove them. What’s more, having an eye from outside the business can shed light on practices that need to be improved. We are also finding that many audits are now becoming legal requirements, like they should be! There are a couple of external audits that we commission throughout our business, as well as some internal audits we conduct ourselves to measure our own progress. One of the biggest external audits we commission is the yearly audit of our Non-Animal Testing Policy.

The policy aims to guide our supplier’s buying practices to ensure they align with Lush’s company values and underpin our commitment to doing business in the most ethical way that we can. Our buying team, and the wider business, have multiple resources to help suppliers with any barriers they may be facing, or improvements they are passionate about. Our relationships are always mutually beneficial and we know that there is so much to learn from communities who have made these ingredients their livelihoods. One source of support we offer our suppliers is through the Improvements & Remediation Fund. This is a discretionary fund, dedicated to supporting engaged suppliers in projects that improve their social or environmental impact, or further our mission to fight animal testing. Suppliers who have shown an interest in making improvements to social and environmental standards, or are otherwise heavily engaged with Lush’s values are invited by our creative buying team to apply for funding on projects that meet our criteria. In FY2024, we carried out 13 projects in collaboration with our suppliers, in which we contributed about £166,000 Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 45 PRINCIPLE: FAIR BUSINESS DEALINGS WITH OUR SUPPLIERS Despite Fairtrade certification and other certification schemes being a useful tool, and a reassuring mark of producers and traders doing the right thing, Lush as a company does not solely rely on these certifications. We find that working as directly as possible with primary producers of our ingredients and packaging is the best way for us to achieve transparency in our supply network, while also making sure that our supplier relationships are fair and built on open communication. The term fair trade, whilst associated with formal certifications, can be expressed and verified in many forms, some of which are far more economically and structurally viable for our producers.

Since 2010 we have been gradually replacing palm-derived materials. We increased transparency with our suppliers and better monitoring through GPS combined with satellite data. In FY24, we reduced the use of palm-derived ingredients by 10% as we moved to sodium coco-sulfate as the base of our shampoo bars. 46% of raw materials by weight that would normally be derived from palm have been sourced from a diversity of different plant oil feedstocks. Ingredients like Tonka Beans, Illipe Butter or Brazil Nut oil help forest peoples to get economic value from a standing forest. Long-term partnerships and additional funding meant 138 families connected to Murumuru Butter benefited from a tree planting programme, with Guyra Paraguay NGO, 20 hectares of agroforestry were implemented, protecting 6,900 hectares of the Atlantic Rainforest and providing livelihoods for more than 50 families. Through our land defenders fund, £200,000 donated to those protecting ecosystems with their lives. Recently, this has also included environmental reporting in line with the Taskforce on Climate Related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) framework. Our Group financial statements are fully audited by a reputable and qualified audit firm which includes consideration of our TCFD disclosures and Group wide internal controls. We also audit some of our buying and supply network practices internally to ensure better visibility across these networks and help, where we can, remediate on any issues we find. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 76 PRINCIPLE: ENVIRONMENTAL POLICIES Retail sits at 54% on average, with many shops around the world without access to adequate facilities for recycling materials - in North America we have been working with shopping malls to improve access. Wet paper towels are the single biggest source of non-recyclable waste for our shops! We ask our shops to ‘weigh the waste’ once a year so we can collect the data and they can get more familiar with what goes on in their waste. When it comes to product waste, if the product is still usable we make every effort to donate it. We donate straight from the store or from our manufacturing centres to various charities. If it’s not possible to donate the product then we send these to composting where we can. In the UK, we invested in our own recycling centre, the Green Hub. It processes the Bring it Back returns from the stores and our UK manufacturing wastes. Green Hub: 81% of solid waste recycled, recovered, or composted.

The full report will take you through how we arrived at these estimations. Our initial interest in making naked products was born out of necessity. After the demise of Cosmetics To Go, the Lush Co-Founders simply didn’t have the money to invest in packaging, but this actually sparked a revolutionary approach to a growing industry problem. We have been selling solid products even in the years before Lush, when we were known as Cosmetics To Go. However, they were not born naked! The mail order company would wrap exciting bath bombs, bubble bars, bath oils, soaps, massage bars and the now famous shampoo bar in sometimes extravagant packaging! It was only when Lush opened its first store – in Poole High Street with a delicatessen vibe – that those products went naked. Our latest invention is the Naked Mascara, formulated with butters and waxes to create an iconic, solid product. This has become a favourite product for our customers and has won multiple awards such as the 2022 UK Sustainable Beauty Awards for Sustainable Product, the 2023 NA Harper’s Bazaar Beauty Icons Awards for Most Innovative Beauty Product and the 2024 Australia Sustainability Awards for the Product Award. We will seek out and favour advances in new energy sources and solutions as they become available. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 63 PRINCIPLE: RENEWABLE ENERGY In 2024, we've hit a major milestone: 100% renewable electricity across Lush operations (excluding partner markets). Through a mix of direct ‘deep green’ tariffs — 40% of properties — and standard electricity tariffs with matching impact Renewable Energy Certificate purchases, many with additional benefits such as community solar, including pollinator-friendly habitats or additional consideration for the impact potential on animals. The heat challenge remains trickier. We're almost fossil gas-free in retail and gradually electrifying factories. Our North American sites run on 100% renewable energy despite still utilising gas, by supporting recovery of natural gas from a landfill project. In the UK,our main R&D building (Unit 1 Witney) and our EC’s/Perfumes factory operate fully on electricity with Unit 1 also being supplied by 126kWp of solar PV directly from the roof, supplying over 30% of our annual electricity demand. Our Japanese manufacturing facility is already 81% electrified. We are currently performing energy audits in Japan, LED upgrades across UK facilities, remaining non-LED German shops, better monitoring, double doors in the new Glasgow store to improve efficiency, and ‘power down’ campaigns for staff.

We encourage suppliers to test for safety using non-animal testing methods and to fund the development of non-animal test methods. Every year, we will request all our suppliers, globally, to complete our Non Animal Testing (NAT) declaration form to ensure they are still compliant within our policy. In an ever changing supply market, constant vigilance is needed to ensure we replace any suppliers who, through changes in company behaviour or company ownership, suddenly fall outside of our compliance criteria. In these instances our buying team will move that supplier to our non-compliant list and will search for a different supplier of those ingredients. Registered number 17 July 2025 04162033 9 PRINCIPLE: ANIMAL TESTING We are also audited by Ethical Consumer every year, to ensure that we are complying with our own NAT policies. We also fund the Lush Prize - a biannual prize, run in partnership with Ethical Consumer and an independent panel of scientist judges, rewarding scientists and those working to end animal testing. We will continue to use every tool we have to fight animal testing until it is completely eliminated worldwide. Between 2020 and 2024 our NAT global compliance* rate has increased by 6%, from 86% to 90% in our most recent audit. * Instances of non-compliance by suppliers include ; failure by supplier to complete or return Lush’s Non-Animal Testing Declaration Forms ; the identification of safety testing that contradicts Lush’s NAT policy ; the merger or acquisition of compliant suppliers by non-compliant ones.
